verdict: Q1 confirmed: a HOMOGENEOUS substrate (alpha=0) propagates the ray dead straight (deflection < 1e-4: True) — linearity is the default, by translational symmetry; no active mechanism needed. Q2 confirmed: a local c-gradient (mass) bends the ray, weakly, toward the slower region (gradient-index). THE FACTOR OF 2: deflection(alpha=2)/deflection(alpha=1) = 2.035 across impact parameters. A scalar 'variable-c only' substrate (alpha=1) reproduces Newton/Soldner 2GM/(c^2 b) (matches: True) — HALF the observed bending, the value Eddington 1919 ruled out. Matching GR's 4GM/(c^2 b) (alpha=2; matches: False) requires DOUBLING the effect, which physically means the substrate must perturb BOTH the reconstruction rate (time / c) AND the grid spacing (space), in equal measure. So the classic test hands the substrate a sharp, falsifiable requirement: a mass cannot be modeled as only a slowdown of Intent reconstruction (that gives half the bending and is dead on arrival) — it must equally contract the grid. THE OPEN QUESTION (the real frontier): does the framework's structure (c as reconstruction rate + the two-level time + the grid itself) NATURALLY produce the factor-of-2 (space and time perturbed equally), or must the 2 be put in by hand? If natural -> the substrate explains gravitational lensing. If by hand -> it is fitting GR, not deriving it. Not yet answered; this is where to keep proposing. NOT novel physics (the n=1+2GM/c^2r effective-medium picture of GR is textbook); the value is locating the exact substrate requirement and the exact open question. Bucket 0 unchanged.
